text:
Abstract

For efficient removal of soluble chemical substances in industrial wastewater by aerobic granular sludge (AGS) technology, it is crucial to understand the potential impact of various substances on the microbial community. Under anaerobic conditions, volatile fatty acids are assimilated into storage polymers to facilitate the formation and stability of AGS. However, the fate of other substances in wastewater and their effects on AGS remain poorly understood. In this work, six representative substances (acetate, methanol, n-butanol, n-propanol, propionaldehyde and valeraldehyde) were investigated in terms of their influence on AGS development and stability at 35 °C.

Methanol was selectively converted into methane and carbon dioxide in the anaerobic feeding phase by the methylotrophic archaeon *Methanomethylovorans uponensis*. Under high dissolved oxygen concentrations during the subsequent aeration periods, this population was retained within the structure of the granules. Anaerobic disproportionation of the aliphatic aldehydes (propionaldehyde and valeraldehyde) led to the formation of their corresponding alcohols and carboxylic acids; the latter were accumulated as storage polymers, while the former were adsorbed within the structure of the granules. Although neither propanol nor butanol were converted in the anaerobic phase, their removal through absorption from the liquid bulk prevented strong aerobic growth on the granule surface, thereby ensuring structural integrity of the bioconstructs.

It was found that the unconverted substrates which are not converted into storage polymers may cause potential instability towards the filamentous growth of granules and thus active mixing is required to maintain stable granulation. Nevertheless, stable granulation can be maintained even when high unconverted substrates exist in the bioreactor, provided that the COD is adequately metabolized or assimilated by the slow growing microorganisms. These results can serve as a guideline for designing AGS processes for the treatment of complex industrial wastewater containing varied oxygenated organic compounds.
